title: Undo, Redo, and Back: Stacks and Queues in the UI
description: LIFO and FIFO made concrete in the interfaces you build every day: undo/redo as two mirrored stacks, the classic bug where a fresh edit must clear the redo stack, the browser's Back button as a history stack, a render/task queue as FIFO, and a deque-backed "recent items" list. Every code trace is verified by running it in Node.

questions.title: Undo modeled as a stack: which edit is undone first?
questions.description: You record edits with `history.push(op)` and undo with `history.pop()`. Three edits go in, in order.

options.text: The most recently applied edit, `edit3` — a stack is **LIFO** (last in, first out).
The oldest edit, `edit1` — a stack is FIFO (first in, first out).
A random edit, since arrays don't guarantee order.
All three edits at once.

questions.title: Which array operations turn a JS array into a FIFO queue?
questions.description: A render/task queue must serve work in arrival order: the first job enqueued is the first one run.

options.text: `push()` to enqueue at the back, `shift()` to dequeue from the front.
`push()` to enqueue, `pop()` to dequeue.
`unshift()` to enqueue, `shift()` to dequeue.
`pop()` to enqueue, `shift()` to dequeue.

questions.title: Pressing Undo moves the popped operation where?
questions.description: Undo/redo is the classic *two-stack* design. You press **Undo** and the operation comes off the undo stack. Where must it go so **Redo** replays correctly?

options.text: Onto the **redo stack** (push) — redo is LIFO, mirroring the undo stack.
Back onto the undo stack.
Into a **FIFO redo queue**, so redos replay oldest-first.
Nowhere — it is discarded, and Redo re-derives it from the document.

questions.title: The classic bug: a fresh edit after Undo
questions.description: The user undoes twice, then makes a brand-new edit. What must `applyEdit()` do to the redo stack to stay correct?

options.text: Clear it — the old redo entries describe a future that is now unreachable.
Leave it untouched, so the user can still redo the abandoned branch.
Move its contents onto the undo stack.
Reverse it, so the redo order matches the new edit.

questions.title: Back, then a new link: what happens to forward history?
questions.description: You visit A → B → C, press **Back** to land on B, then click a link to a new page D. What happens to C in the session history stack?

options.text: C is discarded — a new navigation truncates the forward entries, leaving A → B → D.
C stays, giving A → B → C → D.
C moves to the bottom of the stack.
C and D both become forward entries from B.

questions.explanation:
Session history is a stack with a *current-entry pointer*. **Back** and **Forward** only move the pointer — they don't delete entries, which is exactly why Forward still works. But a brand-new navigation (a link click or `history.pushState`) pushes a new entry and **discards everything after the current pointer**:

```js
let stack = ['A']; let i = 0;
const navigate = (page) => { stack = stack.slice(0, i + 1); stack.push(page); i = stack.length - 1; };
const back = () => { if (i > 0) i--; };

navigate('B'); navigate('C'); // ['A','B','C'], pointer at C
back();                        // pointer back to B; 'C' still ahead
navigate('D');                 // new nav from B drops 'C'
console.log(stack);            // [ 'A', 'B', 'D' ]
```

This is the *same shape* as the redo bug above: pressing **Back** is like **Undo**, and a fresh navigation is like a new edit that invalidates the "forward" (redo) branch.

questions.title: What does a breadcrumb trail actually represent?
questions.description: A product page shows the trail `Home / Products / Laptops`. Which structure does this most directly reflect?

options.text: The page's position in the site **hierarchy** — the path from the root, regardless of how you arrived.
The exact sequence of pages you visited to get here.
A FIFO queue of your most recently viewed pages.
The browser's forward stack.

questions.explanation:
The everyday breadcrumb is *location-based*: it encodes **where the page sits in the information architecture** — the chain of ancestors from the root down to the current page. Two users who reach `/products/laptops` see the identical trail even if one came from search and the other from the home page, because it is derived from the site tree, not from visit order.

That is what separates it from the **Back** stack (which *does* record your actual path) and from a "recently viewed" list (a bounded queue/deque ordered by recency). A rarer *path-based* breadcrumb does mirror history, but it largely duplicates the Back button and is seldom used in practice.

questions.title: Stack and queue operations and terminology
questions.description: Select every statement that is correct.

options.text: A stack's two core operations are **push** and **pop**, both acting on the same end (the top).
A queue's core operations are **enqueue** (add at the back) and **dequeue** (remove from the front).
**peek** (a.k.a. `top` / `front`) returns the next element to be removed, without removing it.
A stack removes elements in the same order they were added — that is, FIFO.

questions.explanation:
A **stack** is LIFO: `push` and `pop` both operate on the *top*, so the most recent arrival leaves first. A **queue** is FIFO: you `enqueue` at the back and `dequeue` from the front, so arrivals leave in order. Both expose a non-destructive **peek** that reveals the next element to come off — the top of a stack, the front of a queue — without mutating the structure.

The false statement swaps the definitions: leaving in *arrival* order (FIFO) is the **queue's** behavior. A stack is **LIFO**, so it removes elements in the *reverse* of the order they were added.

questions.title: Which of these behave like a stack (LIFO)?
questions.description: Select every mechanism whose behavior is last-in, first-out.

options.text: Undo history — your most recent edit is the first one reversed.
The browser's **Back** button walking back through visited pages.
The event loop's macrotask (task) queue, draining callbacks in the order they were scheduled.
A "Recently viewed products" list that shows the newest item first.

questions.explanation:
Undo and **Back** are textbook stacks: both reverse your *most recent* action first (LIFO). The two distractors are tempting but are **not** stacks.

The event loop's task queue is **FIFO** — callbacks run in the order they were enqueued, which is why a `setTimeout(a, 0)` scheduled before `setTimeout(b, 0)` runs `a` first. A "recently viewed" list *displays* newest-first, but as a data structure it is a bounded **deque**: you add to the front and evict the oldest from the *back* — the opposite end from a stack — and you never "pop" the newest item to consume it.

questions.title: Bounded history and a recent-items deque
questions.description: An editor caps undo history at 50 steps and shows a "Recently viewed" list of the last 10 items. Select every correct statement.

options.text: When the 51st edit arrives, a bounded undo stack should **drop the oldest** entry (from the bottom) while pushing the newest on top.
A fixed-size "recent items" list is naturally a **deque**: add the newest to the front, evict the oldest from the back.
Capping undo history bounds memory, but the very oldest edits can no longer be undone.
To bound the undo stack, you should drop the **newest** edit so older history is preserved.

questions.title: Name the double-ended structure
questions.description: A queue you can add to and remove from at **both** ends — the structure behind a bounded "recent items" list. Enter its common 5-letter shortened name.
